subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges Jason's suggestion to simplify Mas_erase() by moving the mas_wr_preallocate() call out of atomic context, agrees to try it in v4 and send a new patch if successful.
reviews.raw_body:
Mas_erase() would look cleaner, indeed, and it
looks like the mas_wr_preallocate() call is
safe from atomic context, as well!

Let me give that a try tomorrow.

I'll send a v4 if that cleanup works right.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-06-10.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed Jason's concern about the safety of calling mas_nomem() while holding a spinlock in remove_iova(). The author explained that this is necessary due to interrupt handlers and proposed two options: modifying the maple tree code or reverting to an augmented rbtree.
reviews.raw_body:
I just looked into this some more, and I was
confused earlier this week.

The mas_erase() function calls mas_nomem(mas, GFP_KERNEL),
which is not safe to call while holding a spinlock.

The remove_iova() function holds a spinlock, with
interrupts blocked, and needs to run like that because
it could be called from places like IO completion
handlers.

That leaves the option of either having slightly
uglier maple tree code, or going back to the
augmented rbtree (but cleaning that up a little).

Just let me know what you prefer, I'm happy to do
either.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

reviews.author: Jason Gunthorpe
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jason Gunthorpe questioned the assumption that mtree_erase can succeed when called from an atomic context, citing missing error handling and lack of checks for erase failures in callers.
reviews.raw_body:
Oh, the kdoc doesn't say that, it doesn't return any error code if it
can't allocate memory, and not a single caller checks for erase
failures.

I assumed internally it "somehow worked out" even though there are
allocations in the callchains..

This is probably a better question for Liam? Can mtree_erase actually
fail ENOMEM? Is it safe to call it in an atomic context?

Jason

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: The author acknowledges that GFP_ATOMIC allocation can fail and suggests simplifying the code by always asynchronously freeing IOVAs through a helper function, but notes that this may not be suitable for all cases.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes, it can fail.

When it does, __free_iova and friends fall back to
asynchronously freeing the iova from a worker.

If we are ok with always asynchronously freeing
iovas, we might be able to simplify the code by
always going through that helper.

If there are cases where asynchronously freeing
the iova breaks the system, we cannot use the
maple tree, but need the augmented rbtree, instead.

I do still have a cleaned up version of the augmented
rbtree, if asynchronous freeing is a real concern.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

reviews.author: Jason Gunthorpe
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that mas_erase() does not propagate ENOMEM errors to the caller, instead returning success, which could lead to unexpected behavior in atomic contexts where memory allocations can fail.

reviews.author: Liam Howlett
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that the patch does not handle the case where GFP_ATOMIC allocation fails to allocate memory for the deferred free list, potentially leading to a crash when the delayed workqueue tries to access the non-existent list.

Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) being documented as impossible to fail contradicts the patch's implementation of a deferred free mechanism for GFP_ATOMIC allocation failures.

Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that the GFP_ATOMIC allocation failure in mas_store_gfp() does not propagate an error to the caller, and suggested returning -ENOMEM instead of NULL, updating documentation, and auditing callers.

The reviewer suggested using XA_ZERO_ENTRY to mark entries as unusable without modifying the internal node structure, which would reduce rebalancing and allocation overhead during overwrite operations.
reviews.raw_body:
+Cc maple-tree list.

On 26/06/12 03:03PM, Jason Gunthorpe wrote:

---

At one point this was considered to be impossible to fail, and it is
documented to return the entry or null.

---

It _can_ fail, but right now that error will not be propagated to the
caller.  The caller could infer the failure due to the return of NULL...
if that's what would happen, so there's very much an issue on failure
that I need to investigate and fix.

It is possible that it fails with -ENOMEM.  And the spinlock can be
dropped if you are not using an external lock and the gfp flag allows
blocking.  On failure to allocate and the lock is dropped, we retry the
operation from the start in case there was a race with another writer.

I think I should probably change this to return -ENOMEM, fix the docs on
it, and probably audit the callers (most use external locks or are
early-boot-so-don't-worry-about-it).  Any issue here should also be
caught by lockdep pretty quickly.

---

mas_store_gfp() works if you know the range of your entry.  You could
also write the XA_ZERO_ENTRY over the entry so that there is no internal
node changes - just a value swap.  If you do this, you have to be
careful when reading things back when using the mas_ interface.

I think, in your case, hitting an XA_ZERO_ENTRY would be necessary to
indicate that we cannot reuse this particular location until it is
correctly dealt with?  Or is the maple tree the only reason it is
considered unusable?

One thing to remember is that each write can cause allocations to occur,
so if you have a list of items being overwritten then you are causing
the tree to do each write and potentially rebalancing (as you shrink the
data beyond the lower limit of the node).

One way around that is to write XA_ZERO_ENTRY over each one as you
deal with your entry.  Then, when you are done you do a single
mas_store_gfp() of NULL over the whole range.  It will be a larger tree
operation, but smaller than the incremental steps.

Thanks,
Liam

2026-06-12.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.

reviews.author: Jason Gunthorpe
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Jason Gunthorpe noted that many callers to mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) cannot handle an -ENOMEM return value and requested consideration for this limitation.

The reviewer suggested storing NULL in the maple tree instead of failing GFP_ATOMIC allocation, and then dropping a ZERO_ENTRY and setting a bit to trigger a tree sweep on the next allocation if ENOMEM occurs.

The reviewer notes that deferring rebalancing in the maple tree leaves available space unavailable and suggests handling this internally or documenting a reasonable pattern for failable erases.
reviews.raw_body:
Locking aside, I don't think most of the callers can handle an -ENOMEM
return at all..

---

That's probably a good option. Try to store NULL, if that ENOMEM's
then drop a ZERO_ENTRY and set a bit someplace so that the next
allocation has to sweep the tree and clean the ZERO's. At least that
shifts the ENOMEM to an alloc side operation where it can be handled.

---

Yeah, it would be be a maple tree issue only. Defered rebalancing
leave space unavailable.

This is a case where there is no sane way to handle destroy
failure. You can't return an error code from dma_unmap() for
example. So the only reason for this complexity is because maple tree
exposes a failable erase to it's caller..

IDK, could it handle it internally somehow? Or at least document a
reasonable pattern with some helpers?

Eg _iommufd_destroy_mmap() is in trouble too, it cargo culted the
no-check mt_erase.

Jason

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.

reviews.author: Liam Howlett
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Liam Howlett suggested that callers can handle GFP_ATOMIC failures by checking mas_is_err() or xa_err(mas) == -ENOMEM, and requested a note be added to the documentation of the erase function.

Reviewer Liam Howlett questioned the choice of a 10ms timer for retrying GFP_ATOMIC allocation and asked why an external spinlock is used instead of an internal lock, suggesting that the decision to use an external lock might be motivated by avoiding the possibility of the unlock.

Reviewer Liam Howlett noted that the deferred free mechanism may not be sufficient because GFP_ATOMIC allocation can fail even before entering the state where the maple tree erase is attempted, and requested further consideration of this scenario.

Reviewer Liam Howlett questioned whether dropping the internal spinlock on the mt_mmap tree would indeed allow the GFP_ATOMIC allocation to succeed, expressing skepticism about the patch's assumption.
reviews.raw_body:
Callers can check mas_is_err() and check for xa_err(mas) == -ENOMEM.
I'm going to add a note about it to the documentation of the erase
function.

---

Why is the retry GFP_ATOMIC on a timer of 10ms?

Also, why is this patch set using an external spinlock only created to
manage the tree?  Why isn't it using an internal lock?  Is it just to
avoid the possibility of the unlock?

---

I think this is even more complicated by the contexts it is called in -
that is, we cannot preallocate prior to going into this state either?

---

Are you sure that's not okay?  The mt_mmap tree is allocated with an
internal spinlock.  In this case, the lock will be dropped, the
allocation will be satisfied and the erase operation will retry.

Thanks,
Liam

reviews.author: Jason Gunthorpe
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Jason Gunthorpe noted that mas_erase does not support GFP_ATOMIC, which is an additional issue beyond the deferred free mechanism for GFP_ATOMIC allocation failures.

Reviewer Jason Gunthorpe noted that under certain conditions, GFP_KERNEL allocations cannot fail in modern kernels, and questioned whether mas_alloc_nodes() can fail if there is an external lock held. He suggested adding documentation to clarify the behavior of mtree_erase/mas_erase when an external lock is present.

2026-06-17.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.
